data.title: I take it back: A California Democrat can be elected president
data.description:
In 2022, I penned a piece for The Hill about why there had never been a Democratic presidential nominee from California, let alone a Democratic president from the state. 

Among other things, I posited that California was “the big kid on the block that everyone loves to hate,” including detractors calling it the “left coast” and other unflattering sobriquets. 

The nomination of Vice President Kamala Harris last year eliminated the first never, but not the second.

Some people and pundits might conclude that Harris’s defeat portends that a California candidate for president may, indeed, face insurmountable obstacles, based on the perceptions of California in the remainder of the country. They would be wrong, in my humble opinion. 

Recent developments in our political history bolster my view. 

Let’s face it, we hadn’t elected a president from New York since Franklin Roosevelt. This is partially based on the fact that New York City, which, in the past, has been called “modern Gomorrah” and “fear city” — is viewed as hugely out of sync with the rest of the country, probably right next to California. 

But lo and behold, in both 2016 and 2024, the country elected a president from not just New York, but from New York City, indeed, Manhattan itself. And not just anybody, but a multimillionaire who lived in a penthouse with a “24-karat gold front door.” 

Oh, and a man with five kids from three different wives, who was caught on tape bragging about grabbing women’s private parts. Oh, and who had at least four different bankruptcies as a casino owner, gambling itself being considered a sin by some in the evangelical community. 

With Trump’s election, all bets are off about what can and cannot be sold to the country at large.

National polls already show California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) with an early lead in the Democratic field — something Harris never had in 2020. An August Emerson College poll showed Newsom at 25 percent, leading both former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg at 11 percent and Harris’s measly 5 percent. 

In Harris’s case, her demise was based less on her state of origin than her inherent weaknesses as a candidate and campaigner. 

In her previous run, she “peaked at 15 percent” support, not even coming within shouting distance of frontrunners Joe Biden and Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.). She also didn’t win a primary or receive one vote in the primaries, because she ran a poor campaign and dropped out of the race before a single vote had been cast. 

Lest we forget, Harris obtained the nomination in 2024 through a fluke; she also didn’t compete in the primary or have the opportunity to prove herself through that long slog.

Perhaps most telling, she didn’t and doesn’t even have her home state solidly behind her. In her first run, a 2019 Los Angeles Times poll showed her with the support of only a paltry 7 percent of California Democrats.

Worse, the same poll showed 61 percent actually wanted her to drop out of the race. In 2024, she received a lesser share of the vote in California than Delawarean Biden had in 2020. 

Moving forward to 2028, a recent CBS News/YouGov poll showed 72 percent of California Democrats believe Newsom should run for president. A mere 52 percent believe Harris should run.

Newsom personalized his recent drive to pass the redistricting ballot measure Proposition 50, and California voters delivered him an overwhelming 64 percent landslide victory.

In my view, the whole conventional political paradigm has been shifted — nay, invalidated — by Trump’s success in winning two presidential elections despite his humongous personal baggage and widespread negative perceptions of his home state. 

His aggressive campaigning, strong personality and ability to mold together a movement overrode the negatives. Anyone who underestimates or writes off Newsom in 2028 should pay attention. 

Garry South is a California-based Democratic strategist who has managed four campaigns for governor of that state, and was an adviser in three presidential campaigns.

data.title: Watch live: Senate weighs Trump’s deployment of National Guard troops to US cities
data.description:
The Senate Armed Services Committee is convening Thursday morning to hear testimony from military leaders on the Trump administration’s deployment of National Guard troops across the U.S.

President Trump ramped up his immigration crackdown last month after two National Guard soldiers were shot not far from the White House — with one dying as a result of her injuries.

Troops have been deployed to many Democratic-run cities in recent months including Washington, D.C., Los Angeles and Memphis. The administration made moves to send soldiers to Portland, Ore., and Chicago, but was blocked by legal challenges. Trump has threatened other cities with similar initiatives, including New Orleans, New York City and Baltimore.

The event began at 9:30 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Watch live: Noem testifies before House on national security threats
data.description:
Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem will testify before the House on Thursday about threats to national security.

Noem last week said the Trump administration is looking to expand its travel ban to more than 30 countries in the wake of a shooting last month in Washington. One National Guard soldier was killed and another injured after being shot not far from the White House while deployed to the nation’s capital amid President Trump‘s crackdown on crime.

Trump ramped up his immigration enforcement efforts after the incident, including implementing the travel ban and launching a review of green cards held by citizens of about 19 countries.

The hearing is scheduled to begin at 10 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: US, Australia and Britain are all in on AUKUS
data.description:
Not since the U.S. and the United Kingdom first agreed to share nuclear materials, technology and information has there been a more significant defense partnership than the one known as AUKUS, an acronym that includes Australia, the U.K. and the U.S.

Nearly seventy years on and now joined by Australia, we are in Washington, united once again in pursuit of peace and stability. And soon, AUKUS will be a new foundation of our shared security for generations to come.

This week, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Australian Deputy Prime Minister Richard Marles and I met at a critical moment in our trilateral partnership. All three nations have now concluded our reviews of AUKUS. The verdict is unanimous: This partnership is too significant, and the stakes too high, for it to drift. We are all in.

When President Trump declared that the U.S. is “full steam ahead” on AUKUS, he captured exactly where Britain, America and Australia now stand together. The time for talk is over. Now is the moment to get the world’s most powerful, most feared attack submarines built and in the water, and to produce future technologies that will give our militaries the warfighting edge — technologies made more powerful as a deterrent because they’re in the hands of three allied nations, not just one.

AUKUS is a powerful response to a new era of threat. Our adversaries are increasingly and rapidly cooperating, from Eastern Europe, where North Korean troops have been deployed, to the Indo-Pacific, where tensions are mounting. And technology is changing the nature of war faster than at any time in history.

For Britain, this is central to our NATO-first approach. Trump has rightly called on European nations to take greater responsibility for European security. And the U.K. is stepping up with the largest sustained increase in defense spending since the end of the Cold War. And when we say we are all in on AUKUS, we mean it.

This isn’t just about strengthening our shared security. AUKUS will be an engine for growth across our three nations. Every pound and dollar invested, every sub in production, means more jobs, new skills, and thriving businesses across our communities.

For our part, the U.K. is investing billions to upgrade our nuclear industrial base and our shipyards, to build up to 12 of the most powerful attack subs ever to sail under the Royal Navy ensign. And in doing so, we will deter Russia in the North Atlantic while freeing up U.S. submarines to deploy across the globe.

Our nations share a long and storied relationship. With AUKUS, we extend our it long into the future. Generations of Americans, Australians and Britons ahead of us will inherit its strength – in the jobs it creates, in the technologies it develops and in the peace it preserves.

We are in an age of hard power and strong alliances. An age of peace through strength, and strength through allies. And America, Australia and Britain are moving forward as one.

John Healey is secretary of state for defence of the United Kingdom.

data.title: Trump pressures Thune over ‘blue slips,’ attorney nominations
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday pressed Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) to end the chamber’s blue slip tradition, which Trump complained has gotten in the way of confirming many of his judge and attorney nominees.

Trump, in a post on Truth Social, bemoaned that blue slips were making it “impossible” to get “well qualified Republican” nominees approved in states with a Democratic senator. The blue slip tradition allows home-state senators to veto presidential nominees to district courts and U.S. attorney offices, giving them more influence over the process.

“It is shocking that Republicans, under Senator Chuck G, allow this scam to continue,” Trump continued, calling out Senate Judiciary Committee Chairman Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa).

“So unfair to Republicans, and not Constitutional,” Trump continued. “I am hereby asking Senate Majority Leader John Thune, a fantastic guy, to get something done, ideally the termination of Blue Slips. Too many GREAT REPUBLICANS are being, SENT PACKIN’. None are getting approved!!!”

Trump has called for an end to the blue slip policy in the past and has been rebuffed by Grassley, who has argued nominees who do not have a blue slip returned do not have the votes to get confirmed.

The president’s frustration was renewed this week after his former personal lawyer, Alina Habba, announced Monday she would step down as New Jersey’s top federal prosecutor after a panel of federal appeals court judges ruled she was unlawfully serving in the role, overstaying her interim term.

Habba faced staunch opposition to her nomination from New Jersey Sens. Cory Booker and Andy Kim, both Democrats, making her confirmation unlikely.

“The Republicans should be ashamed of themselves that they allow this to go on,” Trump said Monday. “Because I can’t appoint a U.S. attorney that’s not a Democrat, because they put a block on it.”

The blue slip issue marks the latest instance of Trump leaning on Thune over Senate procedure. The president has also railed against the filibuster, which requires most legislation in the chamber to receive 60 votes, and called for the GOP to eliminate it. But Thune and others have been adamant in their opposition, arguing it could boomerang back to hurt Republicans.

data.title: Greene: Trump bragging about economy ‘insulting’
data.description:
Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) on Wednesday called President Trump’s positive grade for the state of the U.S. economy “insulting” and a “poor reflection” on the commander in chief.

“Look, electric bills have gone up,” Greene told CBS News. “Everyone’s bills have either stayed the same or are going higher. Grocery prices have not gone down. Everybody suffered from inflation that happened during the Biden administration, and I think the president needs to be aware that he’s a billionaire president of the United States.”

“You can’t gaslight people and tell them that their bills are affordable, and you can’t tell them that the economy is in A-plus-plus-plus-plus,” she continued. “You just can’t do that, and I think it’s insulting to people’s intelligence and I don’t care how many charts he wants to throw up.”

The Georgia Republican continued to lay out affordability issues that Americans facing, including when parents purchase clothes for their children or people pick up groceries.

“Let’s be real: President Trump doesn’t go to the grocery store,” Greene said, pressing the president to “have some empathy and to change his tone.”

“I actually am starting to feel very sorry for President Trump,” she added. “It’s actually a very poor reflection on him that he chooses to speak about me that way.”

Trump gave the economy an “A-plus-plus-plus-plus-plus” during an interview with Politico on Monday. He cast blame on former President Biden for the economy he inherited and said prices have “substantially” gone down.

“Prices are all coming down. It’s been 10 months,” Trump told Politico‘s Dasha Burns. “It’s amazing what we’ve done. If you think of gasoline a gallon, they had it at $4.50, almost $5.00. You go to some of the states, you had it at $6.00. We hit, uh, three states two days ago, $1.99 a gallon. When that happens, everything comes down.”

The president doubled down on his in remarks Tuesday night during a rally in Pennsylvania, calling attacks over affordability a “hoax” generated by Democrats as a means to criticize him.

“You know, they always have a hoax,” he told the crowd. “Their new word is affordability. So they look at the camera and they say, ‘This election is all about affordability.'”

Other Republican lawmakers have also signaled frustration over Trump’s dismissal of the affordability issue.

Sen. Lisa Murkwoski (R-Alaska) said, “You can’t call it a hoax and suggest that people are going to believe it.”

“By the time somebody is at the level that they’re serving as president of the United States, it’s not like they’re going out and doing their own shopping,” she continued. “Trump is not sitting down on a Thursday night and paying his own bills and seeing what’s going on with health care and how much his credit card shows for gas receipts.”

A Politico Poll released on Wednesday found that 55 percent of Americans hold Trump responsible for grocery prices being “difficult” or “very difficult” to afford, with 27 percent casting blame on Biden.

data.title: AI architects named Time ‘Person of the Year’
data.description:
Time named the business leaders behind the artificial intelligence (AI) boom as its “Person of the Year” on Thursday, describing 2025 as the year when the technology’s “full potential roared into view.” 

The magazine pointed to the likes of Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, xAI CEO Elon Musk, Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg, AMD CEO Lisa Su and Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei in its cover story. 

“Person of the Year is a powerful way to focus the world’s attention on the people that shape our lives,” Time editor-in-chief Sam Jacobs wrote in a piece explaining the decision. “And this year, no one had a greater impact than the individuals who imagined, designed, and built AI.” 

Time typically names an individual as its “Person of the Year” — President Trump held last year’s title — but it has occasionally selected a group of people or an innovation.  

In 1982, the magazine chose the personal computer. And in 2006, “Person of the Year” was given to “You,” a concept meant to reflect the rise of social media and user-generated content online. 

Time’s decision to choose the “Architects of AI” for 2025 reflects the technology’s rapid rise and growing impact. While AI has been developing for years, it first burst onto the scene for the public in 2022 with the release of OpenAI’s ChatGPT. 

Over the past three years, companies have raced to release their own models and top the latest innovations, triggering a global competition that has seen billions of dollars flow into investments in AI infrastructure.  

It has also added a new layer to U.S.-China tensions, as the two superpowers race to dominate the technology.  

Nvidia, once a relatively unknown company that produced video game chips, has become the world’s most valuable company with the advent of AI and the first firm to reach a market capitalization of $5 trillion. 

Time also acknowledged some of the growing concerns around the technology, including its impacts on young users. It highlighted the story of Adam Raine, a 16-year-old who took his own life after interactions with ChatGPT.

“[W]e recognize a force that has dominated the year’s headlines, for better or for worse,” Jacobs wrote Thursday.

data.title: Using a fake marriage to skip the immigration line? Bad idea.
data.description:
You think you’ve found a shortcut to legal U.S. residency? Think again. Filing a visa petition or adjustment of status application for an immigrant spouse on the basis of a sham marriage can have very serious consequences.

A marriage is considered a “sham” if the primary reason for the marriage is to get an immigration benefit. Our elected representatives in Congress have passed civil and criminal sanctions to deter people from using sham marriages to evade immigration laws. And the Trump administration is serious about enforcing these sanctions. U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services Director Joseph B. Edlow announced a few months ago that the agency is “declaring an all-out war on immigration fraud. We will relentlessly pursue everyone involved in undermining the integrity of our immigration system and laws.”    

The agency usually does not look into other reasons for marriages. It can accept a marriage for immigration purposes if it is valid legally and the parties did not enter into it primarily for an immigration benefit.

Sham marriages are attractive to immigrants seeking lawful permanent resident status because of the benefits such a union can provide. Immigrant spouses of U.S. citizens are not subject to the 480,000 worldwide level of family-sponsored immigrants. Spouses of legal permanent residents receive preferential treatment with respect to their place in the line of immigrants who are waiting for the availability of a visa. The wait for a family-sponsored visa can be very long.   

But it is a federal crime to knowingly enter into a marriage “for the purpose of evading any provision of the immigration laws.” A conviction for this offense can result in imprisonment for up to five years, a fine of up to $250,000, or both. These penalties apply to both spouses — the immigrant and the citizen or legal permanent resident. 

Congress has established civil immigration penalties too. For example, the law states that no petition will be approved for any alien who, in the attorney general’s opinion, has previously “entered into” a marriage “for the purpose of evading the immigration laws.” Sham marriages also make immigrant spouses inadmissible if they seek to procure visas or admission “by fraud or willfully misrepresenting a material fact.”

Permanent residency status based on a recent marriage is conditional. It can be terminated if the Department of Homeland Security determines within the first two years of status that it was based on a sham marriage. The immigrant spouse may be deportable for procuring a visa by fraud. 

The U.S. Customs and Immigration Services Policy Manual requires the agency to conduct in-person interviews when circumstances indicate that a marriage may be a sham. During these interviews, the spouses are questioned separately and then their answers are compared for consistency. The questions shouldn’t be difficult for a couple with a genuine marital relationship, but they can be nearly impossible for those who are just pretending. 

Such questions can include: 

  • How did your spouse react when you met each other? 
  • How often do you go out to restaurants instead of eating at home? 
  • What was the last movie that you watched together? 
  • What time does your spouse get up in the morning? 
  • Can you name friends of your spouse that you have gotten together with? 
  • What are your spouse’s favorite foods?  

The failure to answer such questions correctly may be enough to provide the substantial and probative evidence needed to establish a sham marriage. And this is just one of the methods used to determine whether a marriage is a sham. Another method is to check documents that ordinarily are in the names of both spouses, such as a house mortgage or rental lease, auto and other insurance policies, checking and savings accounts, and so on.

The administration’s seriousness about stopping sham marriages and other forms of fraud is reflected in the implementation of Operation Twin Shield, which focused on “fraud detection and deterrence” in Minnesota’s Minneapolis and St. Paul areas from Sept. 19 to 28. The agency chose the Twin Cities because they are hotspots for “manipulation and misuse of the immigration system,” particularly with respect to marriage fraud.  

The operation was a success. It identified “more than 1,000 cases that had fraud or ineligibility indicators, conducted over 900 site visits and in-person interviews, and found evidence of fraud, non-compliance, or public safety or national security concerns in 275 cases — 44 percent of cases interviewed.”

The success of Operation Twin Shield is important because it shows how effective operations to identify fraud can be. Edlow has said that it “is the first of many.” Such operations could soon be “repeated in cities across the country.” 

The Supreme Court has held that the Homeland Security secretary has the “authority to revoke an approved visa petition ‘at any time, for what he deems to be good and sufficient cause.’” An immigrant who becomes a permanent resident on the basis of a sham marriage is never safe from the consequences of that fraud. A sham marriage may seem like a quick way to skip the line, but in reality, it just isn’t worth the risk. 

Nolan Rappaport was detailed to the House Judiciary Committee as an Executive Branch Immigration Law Expert for three years. He subsequently served as an immigration counsel for the Subcommittee on Immigration, Border Security and Claims for four years. Prior to working on the Judiciary Committee, he wrote decisions for the Board of Immigration Appeals for 20 years.

data.title: Sanders-backed progressive launches primary challenge against Rep. Foushee
data.description:
Durham County Commissioner Nida Allam (D), backed by Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) and other progressives, launched a primary challenge against Rep. Valerie Foushee (D-N.C.) on Thursday for a North Carolina House seat next year.

“Everywhere you look in our district, people are working, caring, creating, building the community we all want to be a part of, but not in Washington — at least not for us,” Allam said in a roughly two-minute ad.

“This year, our district suffered the highest number of federal funding cuts in the country,” she continued in the ad, suggesting without explicitly naming Foushee that the congresswoman was “silent” as it happened.

“When right-wing and corporate PACs fund our politicians, they buy silence. I’m not here to stay quiet while Washington fails us. I’m here to fight for the people who built this district,” Allam added, likely referring to past support from groups like the pro-Israel American Israel Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC) and a political group backed by disgraced cryptocurrency entrepreneur Sam Bankman-Fried.

Foushee has since donated the money she received from Bankman-Fried’s group to a non-profit and her campaign has said she would decline money from AIPAC this cycle.

Allam made history in North Carolina in 2020 when she became the first Muslim woman to win an elected office in the state after winning her race for Durham County Board of Commissioners. 

Allam served as Sanders’s North Carolina political director during his 2016 presidential campaign and also served as North Carolina Democratic Party’s vice chair. Allam, who previously ran for North Carolina’s 4th Congressional District in 2022 and lost to Foushee in the primary, has said she was inspired to run for office after three friends of hers, all Muslim, were murdered during the 2015 Chapel Hill shooting.

Allam rolled out her campaign launch with several prominent endorsements: In addition to Sanders, she’s also receiving the back of progressive groups Justice Democrats, Leaders We Deserve, Sunrise Movement and the Working Families Party.

The Durham County commissioner, 31, is the first Democrat to primary Foushee, 69, this cycle. The primary comes against the backdrop of younger Democrats challenging older incumbents as the party faces a broader reckoning over generational change. Many Democrats believe that some incumbents have served in Congress too long or are not meeting the moment for the party as it grapples with a second Trump administration.

North Carolina’s 4th Congressional District is situated west of Raleigh and includes Durham. Former Vice President Harris won the seat by 45 points in 2024, meaning whoever wins the Democratic primary next year is seen as the heavy favorite to win in the general election.

data.title: Live updates: Senate’s dueling health care plans come up for votes
data.description:
Senators will go on record Thursday on competing health care plans that try to address enhanced ObamaCare tax subsidies set to expire at the end of the year.

A vote on the Democrats’ three-year extension was promised as part of the end of the government shutdown. The GOP’s plan converts health insurance premium subsidies enacted during the COVID-19 pandemic into contributions to health savings accounts (HSA).

President Trump’s announcement Wednesday that the U.S. had seized a Venezuelan oil tanker drew concern from lawmakers, including Democrats and one Republican senator, Kentucky’s Rand Paul. They raised the prospect that seizure of ship, alleged by Attorney General Pam Bondi to be carrying sanctioned oil from Venezuela and Iran, would further increase tensions.

Trump on Thursday will sign a bill from Congress and host a Congressional Ball at the White House. Press secretary Karoline Leavitt will brief reporters at 1 p.m. EST.

Follow along for live updates.
